title: Modernity Isn't Secular — It's Decomposing Christianity

source.original: The Post-Christian Condition – The Abrahamic Metacritique (Critique and Digest)

body_html:
The standard story of secularization holds that the modern West gradually outgrew its religious origins, replacing faith with reason and tradition with progress. But this framing accepts modernity's own self-description as the diagnosis — the equivalent of letting the patient write their own chart. What actually happened was not a clean departure from Christianity but its ongoing decomposition: the psychic, moral, and aesthetic structures that Christianity had organized over centuries didn't vanish when their theological ground was abandoned. They were set loose, seeking new objects and new channels.

Romanticism is the most consequential example. It is not merely a literary movement or an aesthetic preference but the eruption of specifically Christian longing — Augustine's restless heart — into the aesthetic domain after sacramental channels were closed. The Romantic poet reaching toward the sublime in nature is doing what the mystic once did, with the only materials left available. And because no landscape, no feeling, no human beloved can deliver the infinite union that longing was designed to seek, Romanticism is constitutively melancholic, forever reaching and never arriving.

Vitalism follows the same pattern in a different register. Where Romanticism aestheticized the longing and dwelled in melancholy, vitalism refused the substitution and demanded life directly — Bergson's élan vital, Nietzsche's will to power, Lawrence's blood-consciousness. These are attempts to recover the sense of life as genuinely alive within a condition that has officially closed the world through which that sense was originally organized. The vitalist reaches for life-giving reality precisely because post-Christianity has the atmosphere of a graveyard.

Even the Enlightenment project and its liberal inheritance operate within this decomposition. Liberalism inherits universal human dignity and the primacy of conscience, maintaining both while amputating their metaphysical root, and proceeds in good faith for several generations before the internal contradictions reveal its arbitrariness. The crucial feature is its confidence: it genuinely believes that theological ground is separable from the content it produced.

Calling these formations modern or secular rather than post-Christian is not a pedantic quibble. It is the very move that renders the condition invisible to itself. Until the decomposition is named as such, every attempt to address its symptoms — political, aesthetic, moral — will remain trapped inside the structure it claims to have transcended.
